title: Things 3 vs Todoist for Power users
meta_description: Things 3 vs Todoist for power users who manage hundreds of tasks using advanced filters and rule-based queries.
constraint_lens: You need task filters and query rules that can organize large task lists automatically.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for power users managing large task lists with complex filtering needs.
one_second_verdict.reason: Things 3 fails first because advanced filters and rule-based queries are constrained.

verdict.summary: Todoist wins for power users managing large task lists with complex filtering needs. It supports advanced filters that combine attributes such as labels, priorities, and due dates into rule-based queries. Things 3 focuses on a clean project structure but offers limited rule-based filtering. If advanced filters and rule-based queries are constrained, Things 3 fails first.
verdict.decision_rule: If advanced filters and rule-based queries are constrained, Things 3 fails first.

sections.heading: Best fit for rule-based task filtering
sections.content: You manage hundreds of tasks and expect to filter them using structured rules. Todoist allows tasks to be searched and filtered using combinations of labels, priorities, dates, and projects. Things 3 organizes tasks through areas and projects but does not support advanced query-style filters.

bullets.point: Advanced filter queries combining multiple task attributes
bullets.why_it_matters: You generate dynamic views such as tasks due soon with specific labels or priorities.

bullets.point: Labels and priority fields attached to tasks
bullets.why_it_matters: Large task lists can be segmented and filtered automatically.

bullets.point: Saved filter views that update automatically
bullets.why_it_matters: Custom task dashboards appear without manually reorganizing lists.

bullets.point: Area and project structure organizing tasks visually
bullets.why_it_matters: Tasks are grouped clearly without managing complex filtering rules.

bullets.point: Clean interface focused on upcoming and today lists
bullets.why_it_matters: Daily planning happens without configuring advanced queries.

bullets.point: Natural language scheduling during task entry
bullets.why_it_matters: Tasks receive due dates quickly while typing.

sections.heading: Where each tool can break down

items.fails_when: Your workflow only requires simple project lists without advanced filtering rules.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use Things 3 when a clean project structure matters more than query filters.

items.fails_when: Large task lists require rule-based queries to isolate tasks dynamically.
items.what_to_do_instead: Switch to Todoist for advanced filter views.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: If your workflow centers on a smaller number of projects and daily task lists rather than large filter-driven systems, Things 3 may feel simpler.

sections.heading: Quick rules
rules: If tasks must be filtered with rule-based queries, choose Todoist.
If your task list behaves like a database, Todoist fits better.
If you prefer a clean project list without complex filtering, choose Things 3.

faqs.q: Does Todoist support advanced task filters?
faqs.a: Yes. Filters can combine labels, priorities, projects, and due dates into query rules.

faqs.q: Does Things 3 allow rule-based task queries?
faqs.a: No. Tasks are mainly organized through projects, areas, and simple lists.

faqs.q: Which tool works better for managing hundreds of tasks?
faqs.a: Todoist works better because advanced filters organize large task sets.

faqs.q: When would Things 3 be the better option?
faqs.a: It works well when managing smaller project lists without advanced filtering.
